articles.title: Bihar heatwave: Schools, anganwadi centres told to end classes early till April 25 for young children - Moneycontrol.com
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Bihar
articles.full_text:
Amid rising temperatures and heatwave conditions across Bihar, authorities have directed schools and anganwadi centres to reschedule classes for students up to Class 5, prioritising children’s safety.
The order, issued by the District Magistrate of Patna, mandates that no academic activities for younger students be held after 12:30 pm. The directive will remain in force from April 21 to April 25, covering both government and private institutions as well as pre-schools and anganwadi centres, as per NDTV.
Officials said the decision was taken in view of the intense heat and humid conditions, which pose a serious health risk, especially to young children. Authorities invoked emergency provisions to implement the order immediately, directing schools to adjust their schedules without delay, as per reports.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a heatwave warning for several parts of the state, with temperatures expected to range between 40°C and 42°C over the next few days. High humidity levels are likely to further increase discomfort and heat stress, making conditions particularly dangerous during afternoon hours.
As per NDTV report, districts across northwest, central, and eastern Bihar are expected to be affected, including areas such as Patna, Muzaffarpur, Darbhanga, Gaya, and Nalanda. Authorities have warned that temperatures may rise further between April 22 and April 24, intensifying heatwave conditions.
While the revised timing applies to students up to Class 5, classes for higher grades will continue as per the existing schedule. Officials emphasised that the move is aimed specifically at protecting younger children, who are more vulnerable to heat-related illnesses.
